formulas.name: Content
formulas.formula:
Since the chapter 'Composition and Structure of Atmosphere' in CBSE Class 11 Geography primarily focuses on descriptive concepts rather than mathematical formulas, I'll provide a list of key points and statements in the required format.

```json
[
  {
    "name": "Law of Gases",
    "formula": "PV = nRT",
    "variables": "P = Pressure, V = Volume, n = Number of moles, R = Gas constant, T = Temperature",
    "units": "Pa.m³ = mol.K.J/K",
    "notes": "Used to relate the properties of gases"
  },
  {
    "name": "Dalton's Law of Partial Pressures",
    "formula": "P_total = P1 + P2 + ... + Pn",
    "variables": "P_total = Total pressure, P1, P2, ..., Pn = Partial pressures of individual gases",
    "units": "Pa",
    "notes": "Used to calculate the total pressure of a mixture of gases"
  },
  {
    "name": "Avogadro's Hypothesis",
    "formula": "V1 / T1 = V2 / T2",
    "variables": "V1, V2 = Volumes of gases at temperatures T1, T2",
    "units": "m³.K",
    "notes": "Used to compare the volumes of gases at different temperatures"
  },
  {
    "name": "Gay-Lussac's Law",
    "formula": "P1 / T1 = P2 / T2",
    "variables": "P1, P2 = Pressures of gases at temperatures T1, T2",
    "units": "Pa.K",
    "notes": "Used to compare the pressures of gases at different temperatures"
  }
]
```

Note: The chapter focuses more on understanding the composition and structure of the atmosphere rather than mathematical calculations. These formulas are widely applicable but might not be directly related to the 'Composition and Structure of Atmosphere' chapter.
